name: Arjun Mehta
q1_what_interests_you: I'm particularly drawn to the intersection of autonomous agents and real-world systems. I recently built a LangChain-based tool that orchestrates API calls for data pipeline monitoring — it reduced our alert triage time by 40%. The agent paradigm excites me because it shifts engineering from deterministic logic to goal-oriented systems where the challenge is defining constraints, not every step.
q2_technical_challenge: At my last role, we hit a wall with our RAG pipeline — retrieval precision dropped to 31% on domain-specific queries. I tried three approaches: (1) fine-tuning the embedding model on our corpus (marginal improvement, 34%), (2) hybrid search with BM25 + dense retrieval (jumped to 52%), and (3) adding a reranker (cross-encoder) as a second stage (hit 67%). The reranker was the breakthrough but added 200ms latency, so I implemented async prefetching to hide it. Learned that retrieval quality matters more than generation quality.
q3_system_design: For a real-time data processing system handling 10K events/sec, I'd use: Kafka for ingestion (partitioned by source_id for ordering), Flink for stream processing with tumbling windows for aggregation, and Redis for the hot cache layer. State would be checkpointed to S3 every 60s. The key design decision is the windowing strategy — tumbling windows for throughput vs sliding windows for accuracy. I'd start with tumbling and add sliding only for metrics that need it. Backpressure handling via Kafka consumer group rebalancing.

name: Priya Sharma
q1_what_interests_you: I want to work with AI because it is the future and I think this company is doing great work in this space. I am a fast learner and I am passionate about technology.
q2_technical_challenge: I once had a bug in my code and I fixed it by debugging. It was challenging because the error was hard to find but I eventually found it and fixed it. I learned that debugging is an important skill.
q3_system_design: I would use microservices and cloud computing to build a scalable system. I would use Docker and Kubernetes for deployment. The database would be SQL or NoSQL depending on requirements.

name: Ravi Kumar
q1_what_interests_you: "I'm particularly drawn to the intersection of autonomous agents and real-world systems. I recently built a LangChain-based tool that orchestrates API calls for data pipeline monitoring — it reduced our alert triage time by 40%. The agent paradigm excites me because it shifts engineering from deterministic logic to goal-oriented systems where the challenge is defining constraints, not every step."
q2_technical_challenge: Building a recommendation engine for an e-commerce platform. Started with collaborative filtering (surprise library), got 0.72 RMSE. Switched to a hybrid approach combining content-based features (TF-IDF on product descriptions) with collaborative signals. Final model: LightGBM with 0.61 RMSE. Key lesson — feature engineering on the interaction matrix (recency weighting, category affinity scores) mattered more than model complexity.
q3_system_design: Event-driven architecture using AWS Lambda + SQS for decoupling. API Gateway as the entry point, DynamoDB for low-latency reads, S3 + Athena for analytics. The critical piece is the dead letter queue — every failed event gets retried 3x then sent to DLQ with full context for manual review. I've been burned by silent failures in serverless, so observability (CloudWatch custom metrics + X-Ray tracing) is non-negotiable.

name: Sneha Patel
q1_what_interests_you: I've been building developer tools for 2 years and recently got into agent frameworks. What hooked me was building a code review bot using the OpenAI function calling API — it learned our team's style guide and caught 73% of the issues our senior devs flag. The gap between 'chat with an LLM' and 'deploy a reliable agent' is where the interesting engineering is.
q2_technical_challenge: Scaling our vector search from 1M to 50M embeddings. Pinecone was costing $800/month, so I evaluated alternatives: (1) Milvus self-hosted — good performance but operational overhead was brutal, spent 2 weeks on index tuning. (2) pgvector — surprisingly good for <5M vectors but degraded after that. (3) Settled on Qdrant with HNSW indexing, got p99 latency under 40ms at 50M scale, costs $200/month on a single r6g.xlarge. The trick was quantizing embeddings from float32 to uint8 — 4x memory reduction with <2% recall loss.
q3_system_design: I'd design a hiring automation pipeline as a DAG: Ingestion → Validation → Scoring → Routing → Engagement. Each node is a stateless function triggered by events (new candidate, reply received, timeout). State lives in Postgres with an event log table. The orchestrator is a simple state machine — no Airflow/Temporal overhead needed for this scale. Critical: idempotent processors so retries are safe. I'd add a circuit breaker on the email sending path to prevent spam if something goes wrong.

name: Mike Chen
q1_what_interests_you: What interests me about AI agents is the potential to automate complex workflows. I've experimented with AutoGPT and BabyAGI, and while they're impressive demos, the gap to production is enormous. At my current role, I built a more constrained agent that handles customer support ticket routing — it classifies intent, extracts entities, and routes to the right team. Accuracy went from 64% (keyword-based) to 89% (fine-tuned BERT classifier + rule-based routing). The rule-based fallback was key for edge cases the model got wrong.
q2_technical_challenge: Deployed an ML model that worked perfectly in dev but failed in production. The issue: training data had different feature distributions than production data. Precision dropped from 0.91 to 0.58 overnight. Root cause was a datetime feature that was timezone-aware in training but naive in production, causing a systematic 5-hour shift in temporal features. Fix: added a data validation layer using Great Expectations that catches schema and distribution drift before the model sees new data. Also added shadow mode — new models run alongside old ones for 48 hours before switching.
q3_system_design: For a system processing 50K candidates/year with real-time scoring and async email conversations: - Write path: FastAPI ingestion endpoint → Celery task queue → scoring pipeline → results to Postgres + Redis cache. - Read path: Redis for active candidate states, Postgres for historical queries. - Email: dedicated worker pool (4 workers) polling IMAP every 2 min, sending via authenticated SMTP with rate limiting (100/hr to avoid spam filters). - Anti-cheat: batch job every 6 hours computing pairwise similarity on new answers, results feed back into candidate scores. - Key risk: email deliverability. I'd use a dedicated sending domain with proper SPF/DKIM/DMARC setup.
